frameworks.name: sequential sampling / evidence integration
frameworks.one_line: decision arises from accumulated samples of noisy evidence over time

frameworks.name: parietal (LIP) decision representation
frameworks.one_line: LIP neurons instantiate the accumulating decision variable during choice

frameworks.name: short-term / working memory readout
frameworks.one_line: evidence samples drawn from memory once the sensory stimulus is gone

frameworks.name: action-based perceptual decision making
frameworks.one_line: standard view that integration unfolds in action-selection circuits

core_question: How do monkeys form a perceptual decision when the motor action that will report it is not yet known?
key_assumptions: decisions involve integration of noisy sensory evidence toward a bound
LIP can represent a decision variable independent of a specific motor plan
task-relevant evidence can be held in and retrieved from short-term memory
manipulations: decoupled motion-direction evidence from the motor report via a color-to-target mapping
targets revealed at unpredictable locations only after motion stimulus was extinguished
key_findings: monkeys postponed decision formation until choice targets appeared
LIP neurons represented integration of evidence leading to the choice
because the stimulus was absent during integration, evidence samples were drawn from short-term memory
interpretations: abstract decisions are formed by sampling evidence from memory, instantiated in parietal association cortex
evidence integration need not be tied to a known action; it can operate over mnemonic samples
